name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
حمٓ (١)
```

```english
Ha, Meem. (1)
```

```arabic
وَٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ٱلْمُبِينِ (٢)
```

```english
By the Manifest Book: (2)
```

```arabic
إِنَّا جَعَلْنَـٰهُ قُرْءَٰنًا عَرَبِيًّا لَّعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ (٣)
```

```english
We have made it an Arabic Quran so that you may exercise your reason, (3)
```

```arabic
وَإِنَّهُۥ فِىٓ أُمِّ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لَدَيْنَا لَعَلِىٌّ حَكِيمٌ (٤)
```

```english
and it is sublime and wise with Us in the Mother Book. (4)
```

```arabic
أَفَنَضْرِبُ عَنكُمُ ٱلذِّكْرَ صَفْحًا أَن كُنتُمْ قَوْمًا مُّسْرِفِينَ (٥)
```

```english
Shall We keep back the Reminder from you and disregard you because you are an unrestrained lot? (5)
```

```arabic
وَكَمْ أَرْسَلْنَا مِن نَّبِىٍّ فِى ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (٦)
```

```english
How many a prophet We have sent to the former peoples! (6)
```

```arabic
وَمَا يَأْتِيهِم مِّن نَّبِىٍّ إِلَّا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ (٧)
```

```english
There did not come to them any prophet but that they used to deride him. (7)
```

```arabic
فَأَهْلَكْنَآ أَشَدَّ مِنْهُم بَطْشًا وَمَضَىٰ مَثَلُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (٨)
```

```english
So We destroyed those who were stronger than these, and the example of the former peoples has already come to pass. (8)
```

```arabic
وَلَئِن سَأَلْتَهُم مَّنْ خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ لَيَقُولُنَّ خَلَقَهُنَّ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (٩)
```

```english
If you ask them, ‘Who created the heavens and the earth?’ they will surely say, ‘The All-mighty, the All-knowing created them.’ (9)
```

```arabic
ٱلَّذِى جَعَلَ لَكُمُ ٱلْأَرْضَ مَهْدًا وَجَعَلَ لَكُمْ فِيهَا سُبُلًا لَّعَلَّكُمْ تَهْتَدُونَ (١٠)
```

```english
He, who made the earth a cradle for you, and made for you in it ways so that you may be guided [to your destinations], (10)
```

```arabic
وَٱلَّذِى نَزَّلَ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءًۢ بِقَدَرٍ فَأَنشَرْنَا بِهِۦ بَلْدَةً مَّيْتًا ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ تُخْرَجُونَ (١١)
```

```english
and who sent down water from the sky in a measured manner, and We revived with it a dead country. Likewise, you shall be raised [from the dead]. (11)
```

```arabic
وَٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلْأَزْوَٰجَ كُلَّهَا وَجَعَلَ لَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْفُلْكِ وَٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ مَا تَرْكَبُونَ (١٢)
```

```english
He created all the kinds and made for you the ships and the cattle such which you ride, (12)
```

```arabic
لِتَسْتَوُۥا۟ عَلَىٰ ظُهُورِهِۦ ثُمَّ تَذْكُرُوا۟ نِعْمَةَ رَبِّكُمْ إِذَا ٱسْتَوَيْتُمْ عَلَيْهِ وَتَقُولُوا۟ سُبْحَـٰنَ ٱلَّذِى سَخَّرَ لَنَا هَـٰذَا وَمَا كُنَّا لَهُۥ مُقْرِنِينَ (١٣)
```

```english
that you may sit on their backs, then remember the blessing of your Lord when you are settled on them, and say, ‘Immaculate is He who has disposed this for us, and we [by ourselves] were no match for it. (13)
```

```arabic
وَإِنَّآ إِلَىٰ رَبِّنَا لَمُنقَلِبُونَ (١٤)
```

```english
Indeed we shall return to our Lord.’ (14)
```

## (Commentary)

The Surah is established for the purpose of warning (الإنذار), as evidenced by its opening and its conclusion, as well as the objectives interspersed between them—except for His saying: **Except the God-conscious. O My servants! Today you shall have no fear** [43:67-68] until the completion of six verses, which are parenthetical (استطرادية).

It mentions that the divine sunnah (السنة الإلهية) — *the established way of Allah in dealing with His creation* — is the sending down of the Reminder (الذكر) and the dispatching of prophets and messengers. The people’s excess (إسراف) in their speech and actions does not deter Him from this; rather, He sends the prophets and messengers, destroys those who mock and deny them, and then drives them toward an eternal Fire.

It mentions the sending of the prophets in general first, then names among them Abraham, then Moses, then Jesus (peace be upon him). It mentions various forms of the disbelievers' excess, the foremost being their claim that Allah, the Glorified, has a son and that the angels are the daughters of Allah. Thus, there is a special emphasis on negating offspring from Him, the Exalted; He repeats this, refutes it, and threatens them with punishment. The Surah also contains other scattered truths.

The Surah is Meccan, as evidenced by the contents of its verses, except for His saying: **Ask those of Our apostles whom We sent before you** [43:45]; however, its exceptional status has not been established, as will be discussed, Allah willing.

His saying, the Exalted: **By the Manifest Book** (وَالْكِتابِ الْمُبِينِ) [43:2]. Its apparent meaning is that it is an oath, and its response is His saying: **Indeed We have made it an Arabic Quran** [43:3] until the end of the two verses. The Quran being "manifest" (مبين) refers to its making clear and manifest the path of guidance (الهدى) — *the divine leading toward truth and the straight path* — as the Exalted said: **and We have sent down the Book to you as a clarification of all things** (النحل: ٨٩). Or it refers to it being manifest in itself, such that there is no doubt concerning it, as He said: **This is the Book, there is no doubt in it** (البقرة: ٢).

His saying, the Exalted: **Indeed We have made it an Arabic Quran so that you may exercise your reason** (إِنَّا جَعَلْناهُ قُرْآناً عَرَبِيًّا لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ) [43:3]. The pronoun refers to the Book (الكتاب). "An Arabic Quran" (قُرْآناً عَرَبِيًّا) means recited in the Arabic language, and "so that you may exercise your reason" (لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ) is the ultimate end and purpose of this making (الجعل).

Making the hope of its being reasoned (تعقله) the end of the aforementioned making testifies that it possesses a stage of being and existence (الكينونة والوجود) that the human intellect (عقول الناس) cannot reach. It is the nature of the intellect (العقل) to grasp every conceptual matter (أمر فكري), no matter how subtle or precise it may be. Thus, the import of the verse is that the Book, according to its own station (موطنه) which it has in itself, is a matter beyond thought (وراء الفكر) and alien to human intellects. Allah only made it an Arabic Quran and clothed it in this garment in the hope that human intellects might become familiar with it and thus reason it. The "hope" (الرجاء) in the speech of the Exalted is grounded in the context or the addressee, rather than the Speaker, as has been mentioned more than once.

His saying, the Exalted: **And indeed it is in the Mother of the Book with Us, surely sublime and wise** (وَإِنَّهُ فِي أُمِّ الْكِتابِ لَدَيْنا لَعَلِيٌّ حَكِيمٌ) [43:4]. This is a confirmation and clarification of what the previous verse indicated: that the Book, in its original station, is beyond the grasp of intellects.

The pronoun refers to the Book. What is meant by the "Mother of the Book" (أُمِّ الْكِتابِ) is the Preserved Tablet (اللوح المحفوظ), as the Exalted said: **Indeed it is a glorious Quran, in a preserved tablet** (البروج: ٢٢). It is called the "Mother of the Book" because it is the source (أصل) of the heavenly books from which others are transcribed. The qualification "in the Mother of the Book" and "with Us" (لَدَيْنا) is for clarification, not for exclusion (الاحتراز). The meaning is: while it is in the Mother of the Book with Us—as an inseparable state—it is sublime (علي) and wise (حكيم). A discussion regarding the Mother of the Book will come at the end of Surah al-Jathiyah, Allah willing.

What is meant by it being "sublime" (علي), according to the import of the previous verse, is that it is of such high status and rank that intellects cannot reach it. By it being "wise" (حكيم), it means that there it is consolidated (محكم), not detailed (مفصل) nor divided into surahs, verses, sentences, and words, as it became after being made an Arabic Quran. This is derived from His saying, the Exalted: **A Book whose verses have been consolidated and then elaborated from One who is All-wise, All-aware** (هود: ١).

These two attributes—namely, its being sublime and wise—are what necessitate its being beyond human intellects. For the intellect, in its thinking, only grasps that which is of the nature of concepts (المفاهيم) and words (الألفاظ) first, and which is composed of propositional premises (مقدمات تصديقية) that follow one another, as is the case in the Quranic verses and sentences. However, if the matter is beyond concepts and words and is not divided into parts and sections, the intellect has no way to grasp it.

The summary of the meaning of the two verses is: The Book, with Us in the Preserved Tablet, possesses a sublime station and consolidation (أحكام) such that intellects cannot reach it due to those two attributes; We only sent it down by making it a recited Arabic [Book] in the hope that people might reason it.

If you say: The apparent meaning of His saying: **so that you may exercise your reason** (لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ) is the possibility of people attaining a complete understanding (تعقلا تاما) of this descended Arabic Quran. Therefore, this which we recite and reason must either be completely identical (مطابقا) to what is in the Mother of the Book or not. The second option is certainly false; for how could it be so when the Exalted says: **And indeed it is in the Mother of the Book** and **Indeed it is a glorious Quran, in a preserved tablet** (البروج: ٢٢) and **Indeed it is a noble Quran, in a protected Book** (الواقعة: ٧٨)? Thus, the first option is determined. But if it is completely identical to the Mother of the Book, what is the meaning of the Arabic Quran we have being reasoned by us, while that which is in the Mother of the Book with Allah is not reasoned by us?

I say: It is possible that the relationship between what we have and what is in the Mother of the Book is the relationship between the proverb (المثل) and that which it represents (الممثل). The proverb is the very thing represented, yet the one for whom the proverb is struck only understands the proverb. Understand this.

From what has passed, the weakness of the views mentioned by some in interpreting these two attributes becomes clear. For instance, some said: What is meant by it being "sublime" (علي) is that it is high in its eloquence and clarifies what people need. Others said: It means it surpasses every book by the miracle (الإعجاز) specific to it, and it abrogates other books while no book abrogates it. Others said: It means the angels and the believers venerate it.

Similarly, regarding the meaning of "wise" (حكيم), some said: It is a manifestation of profound wisdom (الحكمة البالغة). Others said: It means it speaks only with wisdom and says only truth and correctness; thus, describing it as "wise" is metaphorical (تجوز) for the purpose of hyperbole (المبالغة). The weakness of these views is evident upon reflecting on the import of the previous verse and the fact that its being made an Arabic Quran occurred through its descent from the Mother of the Book.

His saying, the Exalted: **Shall We then turn away the Reminder from you, disregarding [you] because you are a transgressing people?** (أَفَنَضْرِبُ عَنْكُمُ الذِّكْرَ صَفْحاً أَنْ كُنْتُمْ قَوْماً مُسْرِفِينَ) [43:5]. The interrogative is for denial (الإنكار), and the "fa" (ف) is for branching (التفريع) from what preceded. "Striking the Reminder away from them" (ضرب الذكر عنهم) means turning it away from them. It is said in *al-Majma'*: "The origin of 'I struck the reminder away from him' is when a rider is on a beast and wishes to turn it away from a direction, he strikes it with a stick or a whip to divert it to another direction; then 'striking' was used in place of 'turning away' and 'diverting'." End quote.

**Disregarding** (الصفح) means turning away (الإعراض), so "disregarding" (صفحا) is an object of purpose (مفعول له). It is also possible that it means "aside" (الجانب). The phrase "because you are" (أَنْ كُنْتُمْ) has a hidden preposition, the estimation being "because you were," and it is connected to His saying: "Shall We then strike away."

The meaning is: Shall We then turn away the Reminder from you—which is the Book We made a Quran so that you may reason it—by turning away from you because you are an extravagant people (مسرفين)? Or shall We turn it away from you to one side because you are an extravagant people? That is, We shall not turn it away from you for that reason.

His saying, the Exalted: **How many a prophet have We sent among the ancients! And no prophet came to them but they used to mock him** (وَكَمْ أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ نَبِيٍّ فِي الْأَوَّلِينَ وَما يَأْتِيهِمْ مِنْ نَبِيٍّ إِلَّا كانُوا بِهِ يَسْتَهْزِؤُنَ) [43:6-7]. "How many" (كَمْ) is for multiplicity (التكثير). The "ancients" (الأولون) are the passed nations. "And no prophet came to them..." is a circumstantial clause (حال), and its operator is "We sent."

These two verses and what follows them serve as a justification (تعليل) for not turning the Reminder away from them, by explaining that your being an extravagant people does not prevent Us from carrying out the sunnah of guidance through revelation (الوحي). For We have sent many a prophet among the past nations, and the situation was that no prophet came to them but they mocked him, and the matter ended with Us destroying those among them who were more powerful (أشد بطشا) than you.

Just as the end of their excess and mockery was destruction rather than the turning away [of the Reminder], so too is the end of your excess. In these three verses, as you see, there is a promise for the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and a threat for his people.

His saying, the Exalted: **So We destroyed those who were more powerful than them, and the example of the ancients has passed** (فَأَهْلَكْنا أَشَدَّ مِنْهُمْ بَطْشاً وَمَضى مَثَلُ الْأَوَّلِينَ) [43:8]. Al-Raghib said: **Power** (البطش) is to seize something with force. End quote. In the verse, there is a shift (التفات) in His saying: "than them" (مِنْهُمْ) from address to the third person (الغيبة). The reason for this was to turn from addressing them to addressing the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) because of their failure to take heed of these stories and lessons, and to serve as a preparation for His subsequent saying: "and the example of the ancients has passed." This is supported by His later saying: "And if you ask them," which is an address to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). The meaning of His saying: "and the example of the ancients has passed" is that the description of the ancient nations and how that which they used to mock encompassed them has already passed in the surahs revealed before this one.

His saying, the Exalted: **If you ask them, ‘Who created the heavens and the earth?’ they will surely say, ‘The All-mighty, the All-knowing created them’** (وَلَئِنْ سَأَلْتَهُمْ مَنْ خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ لَيَقُولُنَّ خَلَقَهُنَّ الْعَزِيزُ الْعَلِيمُ) [43:9]. In this verse and what follows it until the completion of six verses, there is an argument for His Lordship (ربوبيته) and His uniqueness therein, with an allusion to the Return (المعاد) and a rebuke for their excess. This is taken from their admission that He, the Exalted, is the Creator of all, then taking aspects of creation that are themselves the governance (تدبير) of the servants' affairs—such as making the earth a cradle for them, making paths therein, and sending down rain. This results in the conclusion that He, the Exalted, alone is the Owner and Governor of their affairs; thus, He is the Lord (الرب), and there is no lord besides Him.

By this, it becomes clear that the verse is an introduction and a preparation for the argument contained in the following verses. It has been mentioned repeatedly in this book that idolatry (الوثنية) does not deny that origination and creation (الصنع والإيجاد) belong to Him, the Exalted, alone; rather, it claims that the matter of governance (التدبير) belongs to others.

His saying, the Exalted: **He who made the earth a cradle for you and made in it ways for you, so that you may be guided** (الَّذِي جَعَلَ لَكُمُ الْأَرْضَ مَهْداً وَجَعَلَ لَكُمْ فِيها سُبُلاً لَعَلَّكُمْ تَهْتَدُونَ) [43:10]. That is, He made the earth such that you are nurtured in it just as children are nurtured in a cradle (المهد), and He made for you in the earth paths and ways that you traverse and by which you are guided to your destinations.

It is said: The meaning of "so that you may be guided" (لَعَلَّكُمْ تَهْتَدُونَ) is the hope that you may be guided to the knowledge of Allah and His oneness in worship (التوحيد في العبادة); however, the first meaning is more apparent.

In the speech, there is a shift (التفات) to addressing the people after the address had been turned away from them to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). Perhaps the reason for this is to show special concern for this meaning in creation—namely, that governance is identical to creation. Thus, their admission that creation is exclusive to Allah, the Glorified, while claiming that governance belongs to others among His creation, is a contradiction in speech born of ignorance; so He rebuked them with this direct address.

His saying, the Exalted: **and who sent down water from the sky in a measured amount, and We revived a dead land with it. Likewise you [too] shall be brought forth** (وَالَّذِي نَزَّلَ مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً بِقَدَرٍ فَأَنْشَرْنا بِهِ بَلْدَةً مَيْتاً كَذلِكَ تُخْرَجُونَ) [43:11]. He qualified the sending down of water with "in a measured amount" (بِقَدَرٍ) to indicate that it is by will and governance, not by chance. "Revival" (الإنشار) is giving life. "Dead" (ميت) is a lightened form of "dead" (ميت) with a shaddah. Describing the "land" (البلدة) with it is because it is a place; for a land is only described with death and life insofar as it is a place. The shift from the third person to the first person plural (التكلم مع الغير) in "and We revived" (فَأَنْشَرْنا) is to show special concern.

When He argued by the sending down of water in a measured amount and the revival of the dead land for His creation and governance, He deduced from it another matter without which monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — is not complete: the Return (المعاد), which is the return of all to Him, the Exalted. Thus He said: **Likewise you [too] shall be brought forth** (كَذلِكَ تُخْرَجُونَ); that is, just as He revived the dead land, so too shall you be resurrected from your graves alive.

It is said: In expressing the bringing forth of plants as "revival" (الإنشار)—which is the giving of life to the dead—and expressing their being given life as "bringing forth" (الإخراج), there is an exaltation of the matter of plant growth and a making easy of the matter of resurrection, in order to establish the methods of argumentation and clarify the path of analogy.

His saying, the Exalted: **and who created all the pairs and made for you such of the ships and the cattle as you ride** (وَالَّذِي خَلَقَ الْأَزْواجَ كُلَّها وَجَعَلَ لَكُمْ مِنَ الْفُلْكِ وَالْأَنْعامِ ما تَرْكَبُونَ) [43:12]. It is said: What is meant by "pairs" (الأزواج) are the categories of existing things, such as male and female, white and black, and others. It is also said: What is meant is the pair of everything; thus, everything other than Allah—such as above and below, right and left, male and female—is a pair.

His saying: **and made for you such of the ships and the cattle as you ride** (وَجَعَلَ لَكُمْ مِنَ الْفُلْكِ وَالْأَنْعامِ ما تَرْكَبُونَ); that is, you ride them. **Riding** (الركوب), when attributed to an animal like a horse or a camel, is transitive by itself; so it is said: "I rode the horse." When it is attributed to something like a ship or a vessel, it is transitive by "in" (في); so it is said: "He rode in it." The Exalted said: **When they board the ship** (فَإِذا رَكِبُوا فِي الْفُلْكِ). Thus, in His saying: "as you ride" (ما تَرْكَبُونَ), meaning "you ride them," the side of the cattle is given preponderance (تغليب).

His saying, the Exalted: **that you may sit straight on their backs, then remember the blessing of your Lord when you are seated on them and say...** until His saying **...shall surely return** (لِتَسْتَوُوا عَلى ظُهُورِهِ ثُمَّ تَذْكُرُوا نِعْمَةَ رَبِّكُمْ إِذَا اسْتَوَيْتُمْ عَلَيْهِ وَتَقُولُوا... لَمُنْقَلِبُونَ) [43:13-14]. **Sitting straight** (الاستواء) on the backs is to be firmly established upon them. The pronoun in "their backs" (ظُهُورِهِ) refers to the wording of the relative pronoun in "as you ride" (ما تَرْكَبُونَ). The pronoun in His saying: "when you are seated on them" (إِذَا اسْتَوَيْتُمْ عَلَيْهِ) also refers to the relative pronoun; for just as it is said: "I sat straight on the back of the beast," it is said: "I sat straight on the beast."

What is meant by remembering the blessing of the Lord, the Glorified, after sitting straight on the backs of the ships and cattle, is the remembrance of the blessings by which man benefits through His, the Exalted, subjugation of these mounts for him—such as moving from place to place and carrying heavy loads. The Exalted said: **and He has disposed the ships for you to sail on the sea by His command** (إبراهيم: ٣٢), and He said: **He created the cattle...** until He said **...and they carry your loads to a town that you could not have reached except with great hardship to yourselves** (النحل: ٧). Or what is meant is the remembrance of His blessings in general, by transitioning from the remembrance of these specific blessings to Him.

His saying: **and say, ‘Immaculate is He who has disposed this for us, and we [by ourselves] were no match for it’** (وَتَقُولُوا سُبْحانَ الَّذِي سَخَّرَ لَنا هذا وَما كُنَّا لَهُ مُقْرِنِينَ). That is, we were not capable of it; and **being a match** (الإقران) is capability.

The apparent meaning of remembering the blessing when using it and benefiting from it is to thank its Bestower. It follows from this that the remembrance of the blessing is other than saying: "Immaculate is He who..." (سُبْحانَ الَّذِي) etc. For this saying is a glorification (تسبيح) and a hallowing (تنزيه) of Him from that which does not befit the courtyard of His Greatness—namely, a partner in Lordship and Divinity. The remembrance of the blessing is gratitude (شكر)—as previously mentioned—and gratitude is other than hallowing.

This is supported by what has been narrated from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the Imams of the People of the House (عليه‌السلام) regarding what is said when sitting straight on a mount; for the narrations, despite their differences, include praise (التحميد) alongside glorification, saying: "Immaculate is He who..." etc.

It is narrated in *al-Kashshaf* from al-Hasan ibn Ali (عليه‌السلام) that he saw a man mounting a beast and saying: "Immaculate is He who has disposed this for us." He said: "Is this what you were commanded?" The man asked: "And what were we commanded?" He replied: "That you remember the blessing of your Lord."

His saying: **Indeed we shall surely return to our Lord** (وَإِنَّا إِلى رَبِّنا لَمُنْقَلِبُونَ). That is, we are going back; a testimony to the Return (المعاد).
